{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_ce2ad36e4c9a431e88389734","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# gribtract\n2\t\n3\tA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder — decodes NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed fields and gridded numeric data, verified field-by-field against eccodes/wgrib2.\n4\t\n5\tNo C dependencies for core GRIB2 decoding (DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41). No shelling out to `wgrib2`. JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which pulls in `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI).\n6\t\n7\t## What is GRIB2?\n8\t\n9\tGRIB2 (GRIdded Binary, Edition 2) is the binary file format used by NOAA, ECMWF, and other national weather services to distribute model output — temperature, wind, precipitation, pressure fields on global and regional grids. It is the primary output format for GFS, HRRR, NBM, GEFS, NAM, and similar NWP (numerical weather prediction) products.\n10\t\n11\tDecoding GRIB2 requires handling: multi-section message framing, grid geometry definitions (lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar stereographic, Gaussian), parameter tables indexed by discipline/category/number, five or more data packing algorithms (simple, complex, spatial-differencing, JPEG2000, PNG), and bitmaps for missing-value masking.\n12\t\n13\tThe standard tools (`wgrib2`, `eccodes`) are C programs and libraries. If you need to decode GRIB2 in a Rust project, your options have been to shell out to them, call their C APIs through FFI, or skip decoded values and work with raw bytes.\n14\t\n15\tgribtract is a native Rust alternative.\n16\t\n17\t## Features\n18\t\n19\t- **Pure Rust for core formats** — no C toolchain or FFI for DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41 (simple, complex, complex+spatial differencing, PNG). JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which uses `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI)\n20\t- **Typed output** — decoded fields carry structured metadata: parameter ID, level, forecast time, grid geometry\n21\t- **Multiple packing formats** — DRT 5.0 (simple), 5.2 (complex), 5.3 (complex+spatial differencing), 5.40 (JPEG2000), 5.41 (PNG)\n22\t- **Multiple grid projections** — lat/lon (GDT 3.0), polar stereographic (3.20), Lambert conformal (3.30), Gaussian (3.40)\n23\t- **Lazy decode path** — parse headers and defer grid data; extract individual grid points in O(1) for DRT=0 fields without full decode\n24\t- **Timeseries extractor** — given a decoded GRIB2 message set, extract per-station forecasts for a list of lat/lon coordinates\n25\t- **Python bindings** — `gribtract-py` (PyO3) exposes the Rust decoder to Python\n26\t- **Oracle-gated correctness** — every release is verified against eccodes/wgrib2 over a corpus of real NOAA files\n27\t\n28\t## Crate Layout\n29\t\n30\t```"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"489c68df-027b-48e3-8fce-1aa8c7a73dcb","uuid":"cb9f12bd-56de-4657-b15d-c09f7a69f6ec","timestamp":"2026-07-02T12:07:46.189Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/README.md","content":"# gribtract\n\nA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder — decodes NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed fields and gridded numeric data, verified field-by-field against eccodes/wgrib2.\n\nNo C dependencies for core GRIB2 decoding (DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41). No shelling out to `wgrib2`. JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which pulls in `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI).\n\n## What is GRIB2?\n\nGRIB2 (GRIdded Binary, Edition 2) is the binary file format used by NOAA, ECMWF, and other national weather services to distribute model output — temperature, wind, precipitation, pressure fields on global and regional grids. It is the primary output format for GFS, HRRR, NBM, GEFS, NAM, and similar NWP (numerical weather prediction) products.\n\nDecoding GRIB2 requires handling: multi-section message framing, grid geometry definitions (lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar stereographic, Gaussian), parameter tables indexed by discipline/category/number, five or more data packing algorithms (simple, complex, spatial-differencing, JPEG2000, PNG), and bitmaps for missing-value masking.\n\nThe standard tools (`wgrib2`, `eccodes`) are C programs and libraries. If you need to decode GRIB2 in a Rust project, your options have been to shell out to them, call their C APIs through FFI, or skip decoded values and work with raw bytes.\n\ngribtract is a native Rust alternative.\n\n## Features\n\n- **Pure Rust for core formats** — no C toolchain or FFI for DRT 5.0, 5.2, 5.3, 5.41 (simple, complex, complex+spatial differencing, PNG). JPEG2000 (DRT 5.40) requires the optional `jpeg2000` feature, which uses `openjpeg-sys` (C FFI)\n- **Typed output** — decoded fields carry structured metadata: parameter ID, level, forecast time, grid geometry\n- **Multiple packing formats** — DRT 5.0 (simple), 5.2 (complex), 5.3 (complex+spatial differencing), 5.40 (JPEG2000), 5.41 (PNG)\n- **Multiple grid projections** — lat/lon (GDT 3.0), polar stereographic (3.20), Lambert conformal (3.30), Gaussian (3.40)\n- **Lazy decode path** — parse headers and defer grid data; extract individual grid points in O(1) for DRT=0 fields without full decode\n- **Timeseries extractor** — given a decoded GRIB2 message set, extract per-station forecasts for a list of lat/lon coordinates\n- **Python bindings** — `gribtract-py` (PyO3) exposes the Rust decoder to Python\n- **Oracle-gated correctness** — every release is verified against eccodes/wgrib2 over a corpus of real NOAA files\n\n## Crate Layout\n\n```","numLines":30,"startLine":1,"totalLines":215}}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_2b63e12f624e426b98985e8e","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# Throughput & Proof-of-Speed Dashboard\n2\t\n3\tThe question this artifact answers: **\"Is gribtract actually decoding at the speed\n4\twe claim — on real NOAA files, correctly, and can I reproduce it?\"** A README number\n5\tis a claim; this dashboard is the proof.\n6\t\n7\t## Design rule: speed is never shown without correctness\n8\t\n9\tEvery throughput figure is rendered *next to* its agreement coverage for the same\n10\trun. A fast decoder that decodes wrong is worthless, so the dashboard refuses to\n11\tpresent a speed number whose run didn't also pass the differential check. Green\n12\tthroughput + green agreement, or it's flagged.\n13\t\n14\t## Data source — reproducible, never hand-edited\n15\t\n16\tThe dashboard is a pure renderer over `bench-results.json`, emitted by the\n17\tbenchmark harness (`cargo bench` via Criterion, orchestrated by `xtask bench`).\n18\tEach record carries provenance so any number is attributable and repeatable:\n19\t\n20\t- `git_sha`, `timestamp`\n21\t- `host` (cpu model, cores, mem) — throughput is meaningless without the machine\n22\t- `corpus` (name, message count, total bytes) — what was decoded\n23\t- per-`decoder` × per-`template_5x`: `messages_per_sec`, `mb_per_sec`,\n24\t  `grid_points_per_sec`, `wall_ms`, and (for gribtract) `agreement`\n25\t\n26\tSchema lives in `docs/plan/plan.md` → Data Models → \"Benchmark result\".\n27\t\n28\t## Views\n29\t\n30\t1. **Headline.** Big numbers for the current build: MB/s, messages/s, grid-points/s,\n31\t   and **speedup vs eccodes / wgrib2** (e.g. \"1.8× eccodes\"). Each annotated with\n32\t   the corpus + host + git_sha so the claim is grounded.\n33\t2. **Throughput over commits.** A time-series line (x = commit/time, y = MB/s) so\n34\t   regressions and gains are visible across the marathon's history. Backed by an\n35\t   append-only `bench-history.jsonl`, so the trend is real, not a single snapshot.\n36\t3. **By data-representation template.** Grouped bars (gribtract vs eccodes vs wgrib2)\n37\t   per 5.x template — surfaces *which* unpacker is slow (5.3 spatial-differencing\n38\t   and 5.40 JPEG2000 are the suspects).\n39\t4. **Correctness companion.** Agreement coverage by template, same layout — the\n40\t   gate that legitimizes every speed bar beside it.\n41\t\n42\t## Live mode — the part that proves it's real, not canned\n43\t\n44\t`gribtract serve --bench <corpus>` (xtask/CLI) starts a tiny local HTTP server that\n45\tserves the dashboard and exposes `POST /run`. Clicking **Run benchmark** in the\n46\tbrowser triggers an actual decode of the selected corpus *now*, streams progress\n47\tover SSE, and updates the headline numbers live with a wall-clock timer. This\n48\tdefeats the \"the numbers are fabricated\" objection: you watch the machine decode N\n49\tmegabytes in T seconds in front of you. Canned `bench-results.json` is the default\n50\toffline view; live mode is the proof on demand.\n51\t\n52\t## Reproducibility contract\n53\t\n54\t- Anyone can run `xtask bench --corpus <name>` and regenerate `bench-results.json`;\n55\t  the dashboard renders identically. No hidden state.\n56\t- CI (iad-ci) runs the benchmark on a pinned runner and publishes the dashboard as\n57\t  a build artifact, so the trend line has a stable-hardware baseline alongside\n58\t  dev-box runs (each tagged by `host`, so they're never conflated).\n59\t- Self-contained: single HTML file + the JSON; no external CDN at runtime (vendor\n60\t  any chart lib, or hand-roll canvas/SVG) so it opens offline and from CI artifacts.\n61\t\n62\t## Tooling\n63\t\n64\t- Charts: keep it dependency-light — a single vendored chart lib or plain\n65\t  canvas/SVG. The dashboard must open as a static file with zero network.\n66\t- UI feedback: mount **Agentation** (`npm i -D agentation`) per the workspace\n67\t  standard, so UI tweaks come back as precise DOM selectors rather than prose.\n68\t\n69\t## Definition of done (for the dashboard bead)\n70\t\n71\t- [ ] `xtask bench` produces `bench-results.json` + appends `bench-history.jsonl`.\n72\t- [ ] Static dashboard renders all four views from the JSON, offline.\n73\t- [ ] `gribtract serve` live mode runs a real decode from the browser, streams it.\n74\t- [ ] Every throughput figure is paired with its agreement coverage + provenance.\n75\t- [ ] CI publishes the dashboard as an artifact on each build.\n76\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"489c68df-027b-48e3-8fce-1aa8c7a73dcb","uuid":"7f8b0ef8-afad-4838-ae30-38cabae59b25","timestamp":"2026-07-02T12:08:12.381Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md","content":"# Throughput & Proof-of-Speed Dashboard\n\nThe question this artifact answers: **\"Is gribtract actually decoding at the speed\nwe claim — on real NOAA files, correctly, and can I reproduce it?\"** A README number\nis a claim; this dashboard is the proof.\n\n## Design rule: speed is never shown without correctness\n\nEvery throughput figure is rendered *next to* its agreement coverage for the same\nrun. A fast decoder that decodes wrong is worthless, so the dashboard refuses to\npresent a speed number whose run didn't also pass the differential check. Green\nthroughput + green agreement, or it's flagged.\n\n## Data source — reproducible, never hand-edited\n\nThe dashboard is a pure renderer over `bench-results.json`, emitted by the\nbenchmark harness (`cargo bench` via Criterion, orchestrated by `xtask bench`).\nEach record carries provenance so any number is attributable and repeatable:\n\n- `git_sha`, `timestamp`\n- `host` (cpu model, cores, mem) — throughput is meaningless without the machine\n- `corpus` (name, message count, total bytes) — what was decoded\n- per-`decoder` × per-`template_5x`: `messages_per_sec`, `mb_per_sec`,\n  `grid_points_per_sec`, `wall_ms`, and (for gribtract) `agreement`\n\nSchema lives in `docs/plan/plan.md` → Data Models → \"Benchmark result\".\n\n## Views\n\n1. **Headline.** Big numbers for the current build: MB/s, messages/s, grid-points/s,\n   and **speedup vs eccodes / wgrib2** (e.g. \"1.8× eccodes\"). Each annotated with\n   the corpus + host + git_sha so the claim is grounded.\n2. **Throughput over commits.** A time-series line (x = commit/time, y = MB/s) so\n   regressions and gains are visible across the marathon's history. Backed by an\n   append-only `bench-history.jsonl`, so the trend is real, not a single snapshot.\n3. **By data-representation template.** Grouped bars (gribtract vs eccodes vs wgrib2)\n   per 5.x template — surfaces *which* unpacker is slow (5.3 spatial-differencing\n   and 5.40 JPEG2000 are the suspects).\n4. **Correctness companion.** Agreement coverage by template, same layout — the\n   gate that legitimizes every speed bar beside it.\n\n## Live mode — the part that proves it's real, not canned\n\n`gribtract serve --bench <corpus>` (xtask/CLI) starts a tiny local HTTP server that\nserves the dashboard and exposes `POST /run`. Clicking **Run benchmark** in the\nbrowser triggers an actual decode of the selected corpus *now*, streams progress\nover SSE, and updates the headline numbers live with a wall-clock timer. This\ndefeats the \"the numbers are fabricated\" objection: you watch the machine decode N\nmegabytes in T seconds in front of you. Canned `bench-results.json` is the default\noffline view; live mode is the proof on demand.\n\n## Reproducibility contract\n\n- Anyone can run `xtask bench --corpus <name>` and regenerate `bench-results.json`;\n  the dashboard renders identically. No hidden state.\n- CI (iad-ci) runs the benchmark on a pinned runner and publishes the dashboard as\n  a build artifact, so the trend line has a stable-hardware baseline alongside\n  dev-box runs (each tagged by `host`, so they're never conflated).\n- Self-contained: single HTML file + the JSON; no external CDN at runtime (vendor\n  any chart lib, or hand-roll canvas/SVG) so it opens offline and from CI artifacts.\n\n## Tooling\n\n- Charts: keep it dependency-light — a single vendored chart lib or plain\n  canvas/SVG. The dashboard must open as a static file with zero network.\n- UI feedback: mount **Agentation** (`npm i -D agentation`) per the workspace\n  standard, so UI tweaks come back as precise DOM selectors rather than prose.\n\n## Definition of done (for the dashboard bead)\n\n- [ ] `xtask bench` produces `bench-results.json` + appends `bench-history.jsonl`.\n- [ ] Static dashboard renders all four views from the JSON, offline.\n- [ ] `gribtract serve` live mode runs a real decode from the browser, streams it.\n- [ ] Every throughput figure is paired with its agreement coverage + provenance.\n- [ ] CI publishes the dashboard as an artifact on each build.\n","numLines":76,"startLine":1,"totalLines":76}}}

A fast decoder that decodes wrong is worthless, so the dashboard refuses to\npresent a speed number whose run didn't also pass the differential check. Green\nthroughput + green agreement, or it's flagged.\n\n## Data source — reproducible, never hand-edited\n\nThe dashboard is a pure renderer over `bench-results.json`, emitted by the\nbenchmark harness (`cargo bench` via Criterion, orchestrated by `xtask bench`).\nEach record carries provenance so any number is attributable and repeatable:\n\n- `git_sha`, `timestamp`\n- `host` (cpu model, cores, mem) — throughput is meaningless without the machine\n- `corpus` (name, message count, total bytes) — what was decoded\n- per-`decoder` × per-`template_5x`: `messages_per_sec`, `mb_per_sec`,\n  `grid_points_per_sec`, `wall_ms`, and (for gribtract) `agreement`\n\nSchema lives in `docs/plan/plan.md` → Data Models → \"Benchmark result\".\n\n## Views\n\n1. **Headline.** Big numbers for the current build: MB/s, messages/s, grid-points/s,\n   and **speedup vs eccodes / wgrib2** (e.g. \"1.8× eccodes\"). Each annotated with\n   the corpus + host + git_sha so the claim is grounded.\n2. **Throughput over commits.** A time-series line (x = commit/time, y = MB/s) so\n   regressions and gains are visible across the marathon's history. Backed by an\n   append-only `bench-history.jsonl`, so the trend is real, not a single snapshot.\n3. **By data-representation template.** Grouped bars (gribtract vs eccodes vs wgrib2)\n   per 5.x template — surfaces *which* unpacker is slow (5.3 spatial-differencing\n   and 5.40 JPEG2000 are the suspects).\n4. **Correctness companion.** Agreement coverage by template, same layout — the\n   gate that legitimizes every speed bar beside it.\n\n## Live mode — the part that proves it's real, not canned\n\n`gribtract serve --bench <corpus>` (xtask/CLI) starts a tiny local HTTP server that\nserves the dashboard and exposes `POST /run`. Clicking **Run benchmark** in the\nbrowser triggers an actual decode of the selected corpus *now*, streams progress\nover SSE, and updates the headline numbers live with a wall-clock timer. This\ndefeats the \"the numbers are fabricated\" objection: you watch the machine decode N\nmegabytes in T seconds in front of you. Canned `bench-results.json` is the default\noffline view; live mode is the proof on demand.\n\n## Reproducibility contract\n\n- Anyone can run `xtask bench --corpus <name>` and regenerate `bench-results.json`;\n  the dashboard renders identically. No hidden state.\n- CI (iad-ci) runs the benchmark on a pinned runner and publishes the dashboard as\n  a build artifact, so the trend line has a stable-hardware baseline alongside\n  dev-box runs (each tagged by `host`, so they're never conflated).\n- Self-contained: single HTML file + the JSON; no external CDN at runtime (vendor\n  any chart lib, or hand-roll canvas/SVG) so it opens offline and from CI artifacts.\n\n## Tooling\n\n- Charts: keep it dependency-light — a single vendored chart lib or plain\n  canvas/SVG. The dashboard must open as a static file with zero network.\n- UI feedback: mount **Agentation** (`npm i -D agentation`) per the workspace\n  standard, so UI tweaks come back as precise DOM selectors rather than prose.\n\n## Definition of done (for the dashboard bead)\n\n- [ ] `xtask bench` produces `bench-results.json` + appends `bench-history.jsonl`.\n- [ ] Static dashboard renders all four views from the JSON, offline.\n- [ ] `gribtract serve` live mode runs a real decode from the browser, streams it.\n- [ ] Every throughput figure is paired with its agreement coverage + provenance.\n- [ ] CI publishes the dashboard as an artifact on each build.\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":14,"oldLines":7,"newStart":14,"newLines":7,"lines":[" ## Data source — reproducible, never hand-edited"," "," The dashboard is a pure renderer over `bench-results.json`, emitted by the","-benchmark harness (`cargo bench` via Criterion, orchestrated by `xtask bench`).","+benchmark harness (`cargo run --bin xtask -- bench`)."," Each record carries provenance so any number is attributable and repeatable:"," "," - `git_sha`, `timestamp`"]}],"userModified":false,"replaceAll":false}}

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_3f76731f8c614f6eb71f1731","type":"tool_result","content":"The file /home/coding/gribtract/docs/plan/plan.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"489c68df-027b-48e3-8fce-1aa8c7a73dcb","uuid":"bc423375-b00c-4d84-b840-177a2c9f672c","timestamp":"2026-07-02T12:08:29.502Z","tool_use_result":{"filePath":"/home/coding/gribtract/docs/plan/plan.md","oldString":"A self-contained HTML dashboard that **proves gribtract decodes at the claimed\nspeed, on real files, reproducibly** — and shows it next to correctness so speed\nis never claimed without proof of a correct decode. Fed by a machine-readable\n`bench-results.json` emitted by the benchmark harness (`cargo bench` / xtask),\nnever hand-edited. See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md` for the full spec.\nThis is the artifact that answers \"is it actually working at that speed?\" — it is\na deliverable, not an afterthought, and is wired in early (Phase 2) so every later\niteration updates it.","newString":"A self-contained HTML dashboard that **proves gribtract decodes at the claimed\nspeed, on real files, reproducibly** — and shows it next to correctness so speed\nis never claimed without proof of a correct decode. Fed by a machine-readable\n`bench-results.json` emitted by the benchmark harness (`cargo run --bin xtask -- bench`),\nnever hand-edited. See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md` for the full spec.\nThis is the artifact that answers \"is it actually working at that speed?\" — it is\na deliverable, not an afterthought, and is wired in early (Phase 2) so every later\niteration updates it.","originalFile":"# gribtract Plan\n\n## Overview\n\nA pure-Rust GRIB2 decoder that turns NOAA/WMO GRIB2 messages into typed metadata\nand gridded numeric data, gated at every step on field-by-field agreement with a\nreference decoder (eccodes / wgrib2) over a corpus of real NOAA products.\n\nThis is an **oracle-first** project. The differential harness is built before the\ndecoder is \"done,\" so that autonomous (marathon) coding sessions always have a\nconcrete, externally-verifiable hill to climb: *find a message gribtract decodes\nwrong → add it as a fixture → fix the decode → harness confirms no regression →\nrepeat.*\n\n## Design principles\n\n1. **The oracle is the product.** The decoder is only as trustworthy as the\n   corpus and the differential check behind it. Harness work is never \"overhead.\"\n2. **Real files only.** Fixtures are actual NOAA grib2 messages (GFS, HRRR, NBM,\n   GEFS, …), not synthetic. An upstream archive already captures these — seed from it.\n3. **Tolerance is derived, not guessed.** Grid-value equality is checked within\n   the precision implied by the packing template (e.g. decimal/binary scale\n   factors), not a magic epsilon. The tolerance policy is documented and tested.\n4. **Every disagreement becomes a permanent fixture.** Monotonic improvement; the\n   regression ratchet is the whole point.\n5. **Sub-minute verify.** `cargo test` (local, cgroup-limited or iad-ci) must run\n   the full differential suite fast enough to drive an unattended loop. Large\n   corpora are sharded/sampled in the fast path, exhaustive in CI.\n\n## Architecture\n\n```\n                 ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐\n   .grib2 bytes  │  gribtract-core (no_std-friendly where able)  │\n   ───────────►  │                                                │\n                 │  Indicator(0) → Identification(1) → Local(2)   │\n                 │   → Grid Def(3) → Product Def(4)               │\n                 │   → Data Representation(5) → Bitmap(6)          │\n                 │   → Data(7) → End(8)                           │\n                 │                                                │\n                 │  section split → template dispatch → unpack    │\n                 └───────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘\n                                 │ Vec<Field { meta, grid, values }>\n                 ┌───────────────▼───────────────┐\n                 │  gribtract (high-level API)    │  iterate messages,\n                 │  + CLI (`gribtract decode`)    │  select by key, dump JSON\n                 └───────────────┬───────────────┘\n                                 │\n          ┌──────────────────────▼───────────────────────┐\n          │  differential harness (tests/ + xtask)         │\n          │  gribtract vs eccodes/wgrib2 over corpus       │\n          │  → field-by-field + grid-within-tolerance      │\n          └────────────────────────────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### Crate layout (workspace)\n\n- `crates/gribtract-core` — section parsing, template dispatch, unpacking. No I/O.\n- `crates/gribtract` — high-level message iterator, field selection, public API.\n- `crates/gribtract-cli` — `gribtract decode|list|dump` (JSON/CSV out).\n- `xtask` — corpus management, reference-decoder runner, tolerance reports.\n- `tests/` — differential conformance suite + proptest/fuzz roundtrip.\n\n## Components\n\n### Section parser\nSplits a GRIB2 message into sections 0–8, validates length/number fields, supports\nmulti-field messages (repeated sections 2–7) and the GRIB2 \"edition 2\" framing.\n\n### Template registry\nGRIB2's complexity lives in three template families. Each gets a registry mapping\ntemplate number → decoder:\n- **Grid Definition Templates (3.x)** — lat/lon, Lambert conformal, polar\n  stereographic, Gaussian, rotated grids. Determines point geometry.\n- **Product Definition Templates (4.x)** — what the field *is* (parameter, level,\n  forecast time, ensemble member, statistical processing).\n- **Data Representation Templates (5.x)** — how values are packed: simple, complex,\n  complex+spatial-differencing, IEEE float, JPEG2000, PNG.\n\n### Unpackers\nThe hard numeric core. Per data-representation template: read reference value,\nbinary/decimal scale, bit-depth; expand bitmap; apply scaling; (for 5.40/5.41)\nJPEG2000/PNG decode; (for 5.3) spatial differencing reversal.\n\n### Differential harness\nRuns both decoders over the corpus, compares metadata exactly and grid values\nwithin derived tolerance, emits a per-template coverage + agreement report.\n\n### Throughput & proof-of-speed dashboard\nA self-contained HTML dashboard that **proves gribtract decodes at the claimed\nspeed, on real files, reproducibly** — and shows it next to correctness so speed\nis never claimed without proof of a correct decode. Fed by a machine-readable\n`bench-results.json` emitted by the benchmark harness (`cargo bench` / xtask),\nnever hand-edited. See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md` for the full spec.\nThis is the artifact that answers \"is it actually working at that speed?\" — it is\na deliverable, not an afterthought, and is wired in early (Phase 2) so every later\niteration updates it.\n\n### Provider probe & selection\n\ngribtract's fetch layer (Phase 7) must retrieve GRIB2 files from one of several\nupstream providers that mirror the same NOAA data with different latency and\nthroughput characteristics depending on deployment location:\n\n| Provider | Hosts | Notes |\n|----------|-------|-------|\n| NOAA S3 | `noaa-gfs-bdp-pds`, `noaa-hrrr-bdp-pds`, `noaa-gefs-pds`, `noaa-nbm-grib2-pds` (all us-east-1) | Direct regional endpoint; no CDN; ~10ms from US east coast, ~100ms from Europe |\n| Google Cloud Storage | `high-resolution-rapid-refresh`, `national-blend-of-models`, `gfs-ensemble-forecast-system` | CDN-fronted; ~3–5ms connect anywhere; TTFB still ~110–290ms depending on distance to US origin |\n| NOMADS | `nomads.ncep.noaa.gov` | CDN-fronted HTTP; index + range-request capable; good TTFB fallback |\n\nThe optimal provider is **per-model and per-deployment** — e.g. a Europe-based host\nshould prefer GCS for HRRR + NBM (CDN gives 3ms connect vs 100ms S3) while a\nUS-east host should prefer S3 for GFS + NBM (direct 11ms connect, 375ms TTFB vs\nGCS's 112ms). Hardcoding a provider is fragile; instead, gribtract probes at\nstartup and caches the result.\n\n**Probe mechanism (`xtask probe-providers` / runtime `ProviderProbe`):**\n\n1. For each (model, provider) pair, issue a small representative request:\n   a. Fetch the `.idx` index file (a few KB, always text) for a recent known cycle.\n   b. Issue one HTTP range request for the first message in that index (validates\n      both connectivity and range-request support end-to-end).\n2. Record: TCP connect time, TTFB, bytes-per-second for the range payload.\n3. Rank providers per model by a combined score: `connect_ms + ttfb_ms + 1000/throughput_mbs`.\n4. Write results to `provider-probe.json` (alongside `bench-results.json`).\n5. At runtime, the fetch layer loads `provider-probe.json` on startup and falls\n   back to re-probing live if the file is absent, stale (>24h), or if a provider\n   returns HTTP errors on N consecutive requests.\n\n**Probe is non-blocking for decoding.** The library itself (gribtract-core,\ngribtract) has zero network dependencies. The probe lives in `xtask` and in an\noptional `gribtract-fetch` crate that the CLI and downstream integrations opt in to.\n\n### Fixed-station point-extraction benchmark\nA second benchmark workload alongside bulk full-grid decode: extract a per-station\n**time series** (one value per forecast hour, across a cycle) at a fixed roster of\n20 US metropolitan METAR station coordinates — the common \"I only need the value at\nthese points, across the horizon\" hot path. It measures `stations × hours / sec`\nfrom raw bytes to assembled matrix, in nearest and bilinear modes, each verified\nagainst the full-grid decode at the same points. This is deliberately an **open-\nended optimization target**: decoding a whole grid to read a few points is wasteful,\nbut GRIB2 packing makes cheap random access hard, so there is a long tail of\ntechniques to try — many will be dead ends, and that is expected. See\n`docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md`.\n\n## Data Models\n\n```rust\nstruct Message<'a> { sections: Sections<'a>, fields: Vec<Field> }\n\nstruct Field {\n    discipline: u8,\n    parameter: ParameterId,      // category + number → name/units\n    level: Level,                // type + value(s)\n    forecast: ForecastTime,      // reference time + offset + statistical window\n    ensemble: Option<Ensemble>,  // member / type\n    grid: GridDefinition,        // template 3.x → geometry + point order\n    values: GridValues,          // f64 grid + missing mask\n}\n\nenum GridValues { Dense(Vec<f64>), Masked { values: Vec<f64>, present: BitVec } }\n```\n\n### Benchmark result (drives the dashboard)\n\n```jsonc\n// bench-results.json — one record per benchmarked decode run\n{\n  \"git_sha\": \"…\", \"timestamp\": \"…\",          // provenance, stamped by xtask\n  \"host\": { \"cpu\": \"…\", \"cores\": 8, \"mem_gb\": 62 },\n  \"corpus\": { \"name\": \"gfs-2026-06\", \"messages\": 4096, \"bytes\": 1734967296 },\n  \"runs\": [\n    { \"decoder\": \"gribtract\", \"template_5x\": \"5.3\",\n      \"messages_per_sec\": 0, \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"grid_points_per_sec\": 0,\n      \"wall_ms\": 0, \"agreement\": 1.0 },          // correctness rides alongside speed\n    { \"decoder\": \"eccodes\",  \"template_5x\": \"5.3\", \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"wall_ms\": 0 },\n    { \"decoder\": \"wgrib2\",   \"template_5x\": \"5.3\", \"mb_per_sec\": 0, \"wall_ms\": 0 }\n  ]\n}\n```\n\nSpeedup vs. each reference decoder is computed from these records; the dashboard\nrenders the comparison and the absolute throughput, both tagged with `git_sha` +\n`host` so a claimed number is always reproducible and attributable.\n\n## Implementation Phases\n\n- [x] **Phase 0 — Oracle harness first.** Stand up the corpus loader, the\n  eccodes/wgrib2 reference runner (on an internal cluster where the toolchain +\n  files live; not the VPS), the field-by-field comparator, and the tolerance\n  policy. Wire `cargo test` to run a sampled differential suite. *Deliverable: a\n  failing-but-green-framework that can score any decoder against the corpus.*\n- [x] **Phase 1 — Framing + metadata.** Section 0–8 split, identification/grid/\n  product metadata. Match all non-value fields exactly for GFS surface temp.\n- [x] **Phase 2 — Simple packing (5.0) + lat/lon grid (3.0).** First end-to-end\n  numeric agreement on the most common GFS/NBM fields.\n- [x] **Phase 2b — Proof-of-speed dashboard (wired in here, updated forever after).**\n  Benchmark harness emits `bench-results.json` (throughput + agreement, vs\n  eccodes/wgrib2); self-contained HTML dashboard renders it with a live `--serve`\n  mode. From here on, every phase's work must keep the dashboard green and current.\n  See `docs/notes/throughput-dashboard.md`.\n- [ ] **Phase 2c — Point-extraction parse-speed track (ongoing, never \"done\").**\n  Add the fixed-station point-extraction benchmark (`stations × hours / sec`,\n  nearest + bilinear, each verified against full-grid decode at the points), then\n  **iterate on parse speed indefinitely** — lazy/partial unpack, geometry +\n  bitmap-rank caches reused across forecast hours, SIMD spans, decode-once-extract-\n  many, parallelism, zero-copy slicing, and techniques not yet thought of. **Expect\n  many dead ends and failed attempts — that is the point of the track.** A technique\n  that proves *not* to help is a valid iteration: record it in the techniques log so\n  the loop doesn't re-walk it. The only hard rule is the project's rule — correctness\n  gates speed; a faster path that changes a value is a regression, not progress.\n  See `docs/notes/station-extraction-benchmark.md`. This track runs in parallel with\n  the phases below and is never checked off.\n- [x] **Phase 3 — Complex packing (5.2/5.3) + spatial differencing.** Unlocks most\n  HRRR fields. The numerically trickiest unpacker.\n- [x] **Phase 4 — JPEG2000 (5.40) + PNG (5.41).** Compressed grids (common in some\n  NOAA products). Pull in a Rust j2k/png path.\n- [x] **Phase 5 — Grid geometry breadth.** Lambert conformal (HRRR native), polar\n  stereographic, Gaussian, rotated. Geometry must match for point asof-joins.\n- [x] **Phase 6 — Ensembles + statistical products (GEFS).** Product templates for\n  members and time-aggregated fields.\n- [x] **Phase 7 — Publish + integrate.** crates.io, Python bindings, and the\n  forecast-timeseries emitter consumed by a downstream backtest join.\n  Includes the **provider probe** (`xtask probe-providers` + runtime `ProviderProbe`):\n  each candidate provider for each model is probed at startup (`.idx` fetch + one\n  range request); results cached to `provider-probe.json` with a 24h TTL. See the\n  \"Provider probe & selection\" component above for the full spec.\n\n## Marathon loop contract\n\nFor an autonomous session to make safe, monotonic progress, each iteration must:\n1. Pick a corpus message currently failing (or absent) — the harness lists them.\n2. Add/confirm it as a fixture (real file, provenance recorded).\n3. Implement/repair the relevant template decoder.\n4. `cargo test` → full sampled differential suite passes; no regression vs the\n   ratcheted fixture set.\n5. Commit. The loop drives two coupled north-star metrics upward, **in this\n   priority**: (a) **agreement coverage** (% messages matching, by template) — the\n   gate; and (b) **decode throughput** (MB/s and speedup vs eccodes/wgrib2, plus\n   `stations × hours / sec` for point extraction) — the proof. A throughput gain\n   that lowers agreement is a regression, not progress. Both are published to the\n   proof-of-speed dashboard every iteration.\n\nSeparately from the phase work, the **point-extraction parse-speed track (Phase 2c)\nis an open-ended optimization loop**: keep trying new techniques to make station\nextraction faster, expect most attempts to fail or regress and back them out, and\nlog what didn't work so the loop doesn't repeat it. Dead ends are normal here — the\ncorrectness gate makes the exploration safe. When no template/correctness bead is\nthe obvious next pick, advancing this track is always valid work.\n\n## Open Questions\n\n- **Reference decoder of record:** eccodes (richer metadata) vs wgrib2 (ubiquitous\n  in NOAA tooling)? Likely eccodes primary, wgrib2 as a second cross-check.\n- **Tolerance derivation:** confirm that decimal/binary scale factors fully\n  determine representable precision for each packing template, or whether some\n  templates need an empirically-set band.\n- **Corpus governance:** fixtures are real grib2 — size/licensing/storage. Store\n  large fixtures out-of-tree (B2) with a manifest + hash, fetched by the harness?\n- **JPEG2000 in Rust:** is there a pure-Rust J2K decoder of sufficient fidelity,\n  or is a vetted FFI dependency acceptable for template 5.40 only?\n- **Where the reference runs:** the differential harness needs the C toolchain;\n  run it on the internal cluster (has files + toolchain), not the VPS. CI wiring TBD.\n- **Scope of metadata naming:** ship a parameter/level name table, or expose raw\n  numeric ids and leave naming to the consumer initially?\n","structuredPatch":[{"oldStart":90,"oldLines":7,"newStart":90,"newLines":7,"lines":[" A self-contained HTML dashboard that **proves gribtract decodes at the claimed"," speed, on real files, reproducibly** — and shows it next to correctness so speed"," is never claimed without proof of a correct decode.
